speaker: interviewer
text: In the interview with Edward Polidi.

speaker: interviewee
text: The thing what was the most important in these competitions for me that I realize later on was actually not the prize that I took. I took the special prize and the golden medal. But one little package that I receive as a present of a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] anonymous donor, donor that didn't left-- he's anonymous. Left his name, and his address, and his phone number.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did not.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did not. Was very little package. Perhaps didn't left it because of fears that will be traced after that by the police, local police.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in Bulgaria?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This was in Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Romania.

speaker: interviewee
text: Romania. Now we're speaking about the '60s was one difficult period of their political life. I received, received this package, but I didn't open, even. I opened after I left the airport in Italy, was where I returned from Romania. And what was my surprise when I saw this very little letter, this from friend. I hope that this music will enhance your repertoire later on in your life with my best wishes. This is music of Jewish composers that disappear during the Second World War. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Of course, this was when this idea for starting this collection that later on I called Lost Voices started. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There were pieces of absolutely unknown composers to me. But nevertheless, when I started to dig inside of the music, I very soon saw that this is one very special music and even more dear to me, not only because was written by people that died because they were Jewish-- we would like to say, maybe, martyrs of the Jewish culture-- but because of the value of the music that I succeed. I hope that to obtain. I hope that some of these music you'll be able to hear now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, this didn't end it with this. This crystallized later on when, during my concerts in different countries, I succeed to enrich this collection with a, a number of other musical works of, again, relatives of Jewish family that you see for one or other, a way to save some musical compositions of disappeared persons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where these pieces titled or authored? Were there any composers' names on them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very few. Very few.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were all by different artists?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many pieces were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There are pieces, perhaps, totaling [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe a couple of dozens. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, I very much would like, of course, that we be able to at least, a few of them, couple of them to hear. But I was hoping that they will remain in the permanent heritage of the Jewish music, of the Jewish culture of European Jews during this epoch. Because this is almost one of the most beautiful monuments to this martyrs of the Jewish culture, of this very dark period of the story of Israeli, Jewish people. I did a number of other things later on in my life. I get possibility to excel in not only with my own activity as a soloist but with my students, that-- already my students started to succeed to winning the national prizes in music. Also I developed this very special program for children's orchestra that gave, actually, the, the live musical life and defined the musical destiny of a number of little children around the globe, around the world. I built around 68 orchestras, children's orchestras, during more than 10 years as an adviser to UNSCO.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell us what UNSCO is.

speaker: interviewee
text: United Nations Scientific and Cultural Organization. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is one new methodology that I introduce, which enabled me to very quickly to not only build an orchestra like that but make capable this orchestra to perform. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was basically the couple of leading world methodologies of approaching the musical education of little children, the first one of which is Orff, the percussion symposium, xylophone and glockenspiels, which enable the children to learn how to read and order theoretical parts of the music. And the other was the philosophies and the methodologies of Suzuki for the, all the little violins, violas, and cellos, the string symposium, which already enabled them to perform. And with these two major components, also adding electronic music, which I compose, which also adding children's stories sometime, from famous stories like stories of by Maurice Sendak that I wrote the musical score of that. And I orchestrated for this show, the orchestra. And we performed with 500 children in the opening concerts in Caracas of the very famous center there, Maria Teresa Carreno-- this is something get to be like Lincoln Center here, perhaps three times bigger-- in front of 15,000 people, [INAUDIBLE] the president of the country. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] by Maurice Sendak, using also his marvelous paintings as a form of moving the top of the orchestra with narration speaker. We hear that one of the top television stars from the local television, national television, something like Bill Cosby here, because the level was very high level of preparing the concert. And 500 children performing, half of them the glockenspiels, xylophones, that are actually after the introduction of piano and other parts, the violins, violas, and cellos, the strings and electronic music in the back. But I had this movie that was given to me by the television there as a present in which you're able to see these young mothers seeing their little three, four years children, not believing how they are able to perform like a finished artist, are able to see the crying faces of these mothers after that, throwing these flowers and the enormous success of this program. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: After that, I tried to arrange something like that here in Staten Island. We succeed to make one concert at Wagner College. But this is where the things end. We will try eventually in the future to develop similar orchestra here. It stayed in maybe New York or in New Jersey, who knows?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But this is so far as the educational activity of mine. Of course, when I was in university in Midwest, I was then professor. I coached there for a few years students in the university that-- we prepared them for the international competition in Colmar, France, north of Paris, where they win also golden medal and first prize as a piano quintet, string quartet plus, plus piano. And this left also my name there in the, in the golden pages of the university in Midwest. Because never before that students win the national prize there from the music department. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] If we turn here to my last activity, this is exactly my effort now to build recordings with this works that I'm calling Lost Voices Collection that already some of this was performed in the concert because of the 50th anniversary of the end of the Second World War and the, the liberation of the concentration campuses in Auschwitz, Buchenwald, Theresienstadt, in the United Nations. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] What I'm able to add to that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, this is where we stand. And I hope now that I'll be able to play for you some of this works written by these composers. I would like to play one I just call 'In Memoriam' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] by [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Ben Susam, Menachem Ben Susam. And after that, perhaps one other little piece called Horah, built in old Biblical form Horah, dance form, a very brilliant piece, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] both, both of them built in the Biblical chants. Of course, at various time I have a number of other things that I would like to play. I-- unfortunately, many of them are with piano. That perhaps for one better opportunities, we'd be able to perform them also and make one recordings with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if you have any questions-- Before we close, before we are graced with the beauty of your music, I wanted to ask you what effects do you think your, your growing up and the experience of living through the war had on the rest of your life? And do you have any message for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Of course, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the effects of, of my childhood over my life was very depressing. This is a fact that's always remain as one very deep scar in my head. Fortunately enough, this wound, it was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] imposed, provoked in my, in my life was not a fatal one totally to destroy it. And first of all, fortunately, I'm able-- I'm alive, able just to speak and recollect everything what was happening. But what I'm very glad is that this [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] left enough straight in order that I start to appreciate it in one different way much deeper. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] First of all, the possibility of a human mind, the possibility of a person to, like every spring, to something bring as new flowers that are able to, to bring something new and something that is useful for the society, the community and to develop certainty there that later on will have such impacts upon so many children around the world. Even until now, when I'm teaching at home, I feel how much beneficial is this findings that I had when I was working with children in the jungles of Africa or in the mountains of South America where I was working with children from parents still living in the primitive society. When I built concerts with this children orchestra performing music of four centuries, with these children, this was from one side [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bringing my confidence of the fact that I am in the right [INAUDIBLE] of one new forms of education or perhaps with this multimedia that I was introducing in the music education as a [INAUDIBLE]. And the others cite this all the time in seizing constant thanks to the life that somehow didn't destroy me totally, completely, because of one or other reason, independent of my personal life. So this is some feeling of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] obligation that I feel now almost to transmit and leave some of these memories of what was in, in my past for perhaps the future generation that they have the possibility to hear and to experience some of the past through some of the music that I succeed to save and perform. Some of these pieces-- by the way, one piece in particular-- it is called 'Crematorium'-- is extraordinary powerfully written piece of music that is enable you almost to live through the experience of the Jewish people inside of the chamber [INAUDIBLE] gas, almost to hear the screams and agony, to hear the prayers of the, the children. This is a piece written in a very contemporary style and [INAUDIBLE] style. And it's almost prophetic because it was written before that, the, the, some of these executions happened. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 'Agony'-- this is the subtitle of this pieces, piece, 'Agony,' written by one of the brilliant disciples of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Schoenberg. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wish that there is little bit time that you'd be able to hear some of this piece. And then this will illustrate it. It'll be stronger words that I'm trying now to describe the music. And music is very difficult to describe through words. It's necessary to experience the, the music itself. So let's leave the music to speak by itself--

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: --through these few pieces that I prepared for violin alone, violin solo.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We thank you very much for sharing your story with the Survivors of the Shoah, A Visual History Foundation.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The piece of music that you'll be hearing now is part of my collection Lost Voices. And that is, is containing music by composers that perished during the Holocaust. Some of the music that those people wrote is almost prophetic in character and merits to take part of the Jewish heritage, not only by the merits of the fact that it's composed by martyrs of the Jewish history by proper, properly-- simply because of the value of the music itself. The piece that now you'll be hearing is call 'In Memoriam' and is written by Ben Susam, Menachem Ben Susam. [MUSIC - 'IN MEMORIAM'] The second piece that I would like to play is so-called Horah, Jewish dancing music form that is based also on some Biblical chants. [MUSIC - 'HORAH']
